points.text: Add per‑IP rate limiting/throttling on login endpoints to curb brute‑force attempts and reduce DB load.
Implement account lockout after a configurable number of failed attempts to improve security.
Optimize the login query: add indexes on username/email, shorten transaction scope, and use read‑replicas if possible.
Enable connection pooling and add retry with exponential backoff for transient lock contention errors.
Shorten authentication DB transactions or move reads to replicas to reduce lock contention and latency.
Add login throttling or IP rate‑limiting to curb massive failed attempts and protect the DB.
Wrap the auth service call in a circuit‑breaker to shed load when DB latency spikes.
Create a composite index on (user_id, ip_address, attempt_time) in the login_attempts table to speed lookups and reduce lock duration.
Separate failed login inserts into a lightweight table or use INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING to avoid locking the main users table.
Limit transaction scope during login: fetch user row, verify password, then commit before logging the attempt to minimize lock hold time.
Deploy a connection pooler (e.g., PgBouncer) and set max connections lower to reduce contention under peak load.
Implement application-level rate limiting per IP to cut down the number of failed login writes hitting the DB.
Set appropriate isolation level (READ COMMITTED) and avoid SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on whole tables; use row-level locks only when needed.
Reduce transaction scope for login verification; only lock rows needed, avoid long-running locks.
Use READ COMMITTED isolation for login queries to minimize lock contention and avoid unnecessary shared locks.
Log login attempts in a separate append‑only table with minimal indexes; use INSERT … ON CONFLICT to make writes idempotent.
Add IP‑based rate limiting or throttling before DB writes to cut down malicious login traffic and reduce contention.
Implement login rate limiting / IP throttling to mitigate brute‑force attacks and reduce database load during peaks.
Move session storage to stateless JWTs or an external cache (e.g., Redis) to avoid DB row‑level locks on login.
Apply exponential backoff and temporary account lockout after a configurable number of failed attempts.
Add CAPTCHA or other challenge‑response after multiple failures to block bots and cut unnecessary DB queries.
Add a front‑end rate‑limit (e.g., token bucket) per IP/user to cap login attempts during peaks, reducing DB load.
Deploy credential‑stuffing detection (high‑failure, many IPs) and auto‑block offending IPs or require CAPTCHA.
Introduce exponential back‑off or progressive delay after each failed login to slow attackers and lower contention.
Cache successful credential lookups or use a read‑replica for authentication queries to alleviate lock contention.
Implement step‑up authentication (CAPTCHA or MFA) only for suspicious logins, balancing user friction and security.
